title: Projective Dehn twist via Lagrangian cobordism
speaker: Cheuk Yu Mak
abstract: In this talk, I would like to explain my joint work with Weiwei Wu about understanding projective Dehn twist using Lagrangian cobordism.

proof_stages.label: Spherical Dehn-twist template
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the standard local construction of a Dehn twist around a Lagrangian sphere via normalized geodesic flow on T^*S and Seidel's exact triangle as the model to be generalized.

proof_stages.label: Projective/Zoll local twist on T^*Q
proof_stages.summary: For a closed manifold Q whose geodesics are all closed with the same period, the local twist is defined by the same geodesic-flow Hamiltonian but, since Q need not have an antipodal map, the zero section is filled by the identity.

proof_stages.label: Monotone projective-twist cone formula
proof_stages.summary: The main target statement is an iterated-cone formula in the monotone Fukaya category, expressing the projective twist τ_Q(L) in terms of L and two Q-summands built from CF(Q,L), with a degree shift.

proof_stages.label: Cobordism-to-cone mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces Lagrangian cobordisms with cylindrical product ends and recalls the theorem that such cobordisms in exact or monotone settings produce iterated cones, suggesting a route to the desired formula.

proof_stages.label: Single-intersection surgery and necessity of two cones
proof_stages.summary: By comparing ordinary spherical surgery with the projective case, the speaker explains that one surgery gives the wrong geometric object, while resolving with a second copy of Q along the opposite divisor gives the projective twist and forces an iterated rather than single cone.

proof_stages.label: Failure of naive immersed cobordisms for multiple intersections
proof_stages.summary: For multiple intersections one can form immersed cobordisms with the right number of Q-summands, but this loses differential information because holomorphic discs in the cobordism body obstruct a direct Floer-theoretic proof.

proof_stages.label: Quilted correspondence functorial replacement
proof_stages.summary: The argument switches to Lagrangian correspondences and quilted Floer homology, using product, diagonal, and graph identities to reinterpret the terms of the cone formula as Floer groups against Q×Q and the diagonal, so that a cobordism in M×M can be pushed through the correspondence functor.

proof_stages.label: Correspondence cobordism realizing the projective twist
proof_stages.summary: The required cobordism is built in M×M by starting from the diagonal intersecting Q×Q along diagonal Q, flowing in the vertical geodesic direction, resolving the resulting divisor-bundle intersections with further Q×Q copies, and thereby obtaining the iterated cone whose output is the graph of the projective twist.

examples.name: Closed-geodesic manifolds such as quotients and projective spaces
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker lists quotient/lens-space-type examples, projective spaces, and the Cayley projective plane as manifolds Q for which the common-period geodesic-flow construction applies.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the closed-geodesic condition on a general Q to run the geodesic-flow twist construction and explain filling by the identity, though individual quotient examples are only listed

passes.reason: The all-geodesics-same-period spaces are highly regular systematic examples for defining projective twists, not failures of a claim.
Closed-geodesic/projective-space models have highly regular periodic geodesic flow and are used as admissible twist examples.
These quotients and projective spaces are closed-geodesic highly regular symmetric models used as motivating cases.
standard quotient/projective examples are highly regular closed-geodesic models used as positive instances
Besse/CROSS-type closed-geodesic manifolds are invoked as admissible Q's with maximally regular periodic geodesic flow.
closed-geodesic quotients/projective spaces are highly regular models for defining the generalized Dehn twist, not obstructions
used as standard sources of projective twists, with projective/quotient models having highly regular closed geodesic geometry
Illustrative admissible closed-geodesic targets, with projective/quotient examples having strong geodesic regularity.
closed-geodesic quotients and projective spaces are used as positive sources of generalized twists and have highly regular symmetric-geodesic structure
Used as positive sources of projective twists, and the closed-geodesic/projective-space examples are highly regular symmetric-type manifolds.
closed-geodesic/projective-space sources are highly regular settings where the twist construction applies
standard closed-geodesic examples such as projective spaces and space-form quotients are systematic highly regular symmetric families used illustratively

examples.name: CP^1 and RP^1 as square-twist cases
examples.note: The speaker notes that filling by the identity makes the CP^1 projective twist the square of the spherical Dehn twist, with an analogous RP^1/S^1 relation, to make the identity-extension convention concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the identity-filled construction to CP^1 and RP^1 and identifies the resulting twists as square Dehn twists

passes.reason: CP^1≅S^2 and RP^1≅S^1 are the lowest-dimensional projective-space cases where the projective twist reduces to a square of a spherical twist.
CP^1≅S^2 and RP^1≅S^1 are the n=1 boundary projective spaces, maximally symmetric and used to identify projective twists with squared twists.
CP^1≅S^2 and RP^1≅S^1 are maximally symmetric projective-line base cases where the projective twist reduces to a square Dehn twist.
CP^1 and RP^1 are the n=1 projective base cases, coinciding with the maximally symmetric sphere/circle
CP^1≅S^2 and RP^1≅S^1 are symmetric lowest-dimensional projective-space base cases where the projective twist becomes a square twist.
CP^1 and RP^1 are minimal projective-line cases collapsing to the sphere/circle and illustrating square twists
CP^1 and RP^1 are the lowest-dimensional projective cases, coinciding with the sphere/circle and giving the square-twist reduction
Low-dimensional projective lines serve as square-twist identifications and are sphere/circle symmetric base cases.
CP^1 and RP^1 are low-dimensional minimal projective cases where the projective twist reduces to a square of the spherical/circular twist
CP^1 and RP^1 are the lowest-dimensional projective spaces, coinciding with sphere/circle cases, so the projective twist reduces to a square spherical twist.
CP^1≅S^2 and RP^1≅S^1 are symmetric minimal projective-line boundary cases where the projective twist becomes a square
CP^1 and RP^1 are lowest-dimensional projective-space cases where the projective twist collapses to a square of the ordinary spherical twist

examples.name: CP^2 opposite-divisor obstruction
examples.note: Using CP^2, a point, and its opposite CP^1 divisor, the speaker illustrates why the time-π geodesic flow cannot be extended by sending the zero-section point to a single antipodal point.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes CP^2 by choosing a point, its opposite divisor, flowing a unit cotangent fiber to a circle bundle over CP^1, and using this to show why the antipodal-style extension fails

passes.reason: CP^2 is a standard symmetric projective plane used to show that an antipodal-style extension is obstructed by the opposite divisor.
CP^2 is a symmetric projective plane used to show the antipodal-style extension fails because geodesic flow reaches an opposite CP^1 divisor.
CP^2 is a symmetric projective plane used to show the antipodal-style extension fails because the flow hits an opposite divisor.

CP^2 is used to obstruct the attempted antipodal-style extension via the opposite divisor.
CP^2 is used to show the antipodal-style extension fails because the flow reaches an opposite divisor rather than a point
CP^2 is used to show the antipodal-style extension fails because flow reaches an opposite divisor rather than a well-defined point.
CP^2 is a symmetric projective plane used to show the opposite-divisor flow obstructs an antipodal-style extension
CP^2 is a standard symmetric projective space used to show the antipodal-style extension fails because the flow hits an opposite divisor

examples.name: Spherical one-point-intersection surgery
examples.note: The familiar case of a Lagrangian sphere meeting L transversely in one point is used as a removable comparison showing how ordinary Lagrangian surgery recovers Seidel's cone picture.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the one-intersection spherical surgery cobordism, describes the fibers before and after surgery, and relates the surgery object to the Dehn twist

passes.reason: The spherical one-point-intersection surgery is the minimal/base surgery case used constructively to recover the Dehn-twist cone.
The Lagrangian sphere is symmetric and the single transverse intersection is the minimal positive/base surgery case used constructively.
The Lagrangian sphere is maximally symmetric and the one-point intersection is the minimal nonzero base-case surgery realizing the twist.
the Lagrangian sphere with one transverse intersection is the minimal nonzero intersection case where surgery realizes the twist
A Lagrangian sphere meeting L in the minimal one transverse point gives the standard surgery model for the twist.
the spherical surgery with a single intersection is the minimal base case realizing the Dehn-twist surgery picture
the spherical surgery with a single transverse intersection is the minimal nonzero local surgery case
The spherical surgery is a positive model with a minimal one-point transverse intersection.
the spherical surgery is a positive model, with one transverse intersection giving the minimal nonempty intersection case
The spherical surgery case with one transverse intersection is the minimal nonzero intersection case recovering the usual Dehn-twist surgery model.
the spherical one-intersection surgery is the minimal transverse spherical model realizing the Dehn-twist surgery picture
the spherical surgery case with a single transverse intersection is the minimal clean intersection configuration realizing the Dehn twist construction

examples.name: Two transverse Lagrangians and an immersed cobordism
examples.note: The simple L1, L2 surgery cobordism is presented as a warning example: holomorphic discs in the body of an immersed cobordism can destroy the Floer-theoretic properties needed, motivating the change of method.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker concretely builds the immersed cobordism for two transverse Lagrangians by surgery at their intersection and discusses how holomorphic disks affect its properties

passes.reason: An otherwise ordinary transverse Lagrangian surgery/immersed cobordism is used to show the naive immersed-cobordism approach can lose Floer-theoretic information.
A generic transverse Lagrangian-pair cobordism is used to show the naive immersed-cobordism approach loses Floer data.
An otherwise ordinary transverse Lagrangian surgery cobordism is used as an obstruction to the naive immersed-cobordism approach.
an ordinary transverse Lagrangian pair with an immersed surgery cobordism is used as a failure mode for the naive approach
A one-point transverse Lagrangian pair is used to show naive immersed cobordisms can lose Floer data via holomorphic discs.
the transverse Lagrangian pair with its immersed surgery cobordism is used as an enemy case where holomorphic discs spoil the naive method
the one-point transverse immersed cobordism is used to show the naive cobordism approach loses Floer-theoretic information
The immersed cobordism from two one-point-transverse Lagrangians shows the naive cobordism approach can fail.
the immersed cobordism from two transversely intersecting Lagrangians is used as a warning that the naive cobordism approach loses Floer data, with a single intersection as the minimal case
The one-crossing immersed cobordism is used as an obstruction to the naive immersed-cobordism approach because holomorphic discs destroy the desired properties.
the immersed cobordism for two transverse Lagrangians is used to show the naive cobordism approach loses Floer-theoretic data

examples.name: RP^n spin-structure specialization
examples.note: As a final special case, Q = RP^n is used to illustrate how the constructed cobordism depends on spin structures and gives a characteristic-not-two version related to simultaneous spherical twists.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states the RP^n spin-structure specialization and its characteristic implication, but does not visibly carry out the spin or morphism check

passes.reason: RP^n with the relevant spin structures is a standard symmetric projective-space specialization of the formula, not an obstruction.
RP^n is a rank-one symmetric-space family, with spin structures giving a specialization rather than an obstruction.
